Yes, it is possible to differentiate between running your Flask app as a server and running a CLI command, and you can pass parameters to the `create_app` function when running the CLI command. However, the standard Flask CLI setup doesn't allow for passing parameters directly to the `create_app` function when running a CLI command. That said, you can use the `flask` command-line interface to pass arguments to your CLI commands, which can then be used to modify the behavior of your app. This means you can still run `flask my_command` without an environment variable, but you can still pass parameters to your CLI commands through the command line interface. For example, you can run `flask my_command x` and then use the `x` argument in your CLI command to determine the behavior of your app. This way, you can avoid the need for an environment variable and still pass parameters when running a CLI command.

If you want to run a different `create_app` function when running a CLI command, you can use the `flask` command-line interface to call your CLI command and then use the `create_app` function with different configurations. This can be achieved by using the `@app.cli.command` decorator to define your CLI command and then using the `create_app` function with different parameters based on the command being executed. You can also use the `flask` command-line interface to pass parameters to your CLI command and use those parameters to modify the behavior of your app. This means you can run `flask my_command x` and then use the `x` argument in your CLI command to determine the behavior of your app. This way, you can avoid the need for an environment variable and still pass parameters when running a CLI command.

To do this, you can define your CLI command with a `@app.cli.command` decorator and then use the `create_app` function with different parameters based on the command being executed. This allows you to run different instances of your app with different configurations when running a CLI command.
